items.title: Introducing view-transitions-toolkit ..
items.description: Over the past years, I’ve published a bunch of View Transitions contents: articles, talks, demos, etc. I’ve also done a bunch of more experimental things with them, such as optimizing the keyframes or driving a View Transition by scroll.What I noticed while doing all those experiments is that I repeated a lot of the code … and they were scattered a bit all over the place as well. So I bundled that all up in a package: view-transitions-toolkit.
items.titleOriginal: Introducing view-transitions-toolkit, a collection of utility functions to more easily work with View Transitions.

items.title: Disasters I've seen in a microservices world
items.description: When Martin Fowler's post about microservices came out in 2014, the teams where I worked were already building service-oriented architectures. That post and the subsequent hype made their way into almost every software team in the world. The "Netflix OSS stack" was the coolest thing back then, allowing engineers worldwide to leverage N...
items.titleOriginal: Disasters I've seen in a microservices world
items.content:
@@@cardlink
url: https://world.hey.com/joaoqalves/disasters-i-ve-seen-in-a-microservices-world-part-ii-9e6826bf
title: "Disasters I've seen in a microservices world, part II"
description: "When I first wrote about microservice disasters, I thought we'd eventually \"solve\" them, with better tooling, frameworks, and operational maturity. We didn't. We just learned to live with the chaos. Distributed systems will always surprise you: timeouts, retries, and fallacies don't disappear; they just shift shape. Maybe that's the re..."

items.title: Using CSS animations as state machines to remember focus and hover states with CSS only
items.description: I discovered this CSS-only trick to store the past <code>:focus</code> or <code>:hover</code> state of any element. Without JavaScript, you can style elements depending on whether they were ever focused or hovered. Here's how it works.
items.titleOriginal: Patrick  - Using CSS animations as state machines to remember focus and hover states with CSS only

items.title: Squash and Stretch
items.description: Have you ever heard of Disney’s 12 Basic Principles of Animation? In this tutorial, we’ll explore how we can use the very first principle to create SVG micro-interactions that feel way more natural and believable. It’s one of those small things that has a big impact.
items.titleOriginal: Squash and Stretch • Josh W. Comeau

items.title: Quick and Dirty Colour Palettes using color-mix
items.description: Learn how to use CSS color-mix() to create tints, shades, opacity variants, and even dynamic palettes for your projects. From simple setups to advanced techniques, this guide explores how to enhance your design system and streamline theming with modern CSS tools.
items.titleOriginal: Quick and Dirty Colour Palettes using color-mix() Always Twisted
items.content:
@@@cardlink

title: "color-mix() CSS function - CSS | MDN"
description: "The color-mix() functional notation takes one or more <color> values and returns the result of mixing them in a given colorspace by a given amount."

items.title: Multi-stroke text effect in CSS
items.description: I used to see that retro multi-stroke text effect quite often and    tried to replicate it using the CSS text-stroke property,    but the results never quite matched.    Because text-stroke accepts a single value, stacking elements was the only workaround I could think of, though it didn't seem to work.

items.title: Scroll-Driven Animations
items.description: The new Animation Timeline API allows us to create dynamic scroll animations without any JavaScript! It’s honestly a very lovely API, and in this blog post, we’ll explore some of the super cool things we can do with it.
